coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive ratiometric fluorosensor for enteroviral RNA detection based on improved electron transfer between carbon dots and ethidium bromide
coredata.dc:creator: Pathak, Abhishek
Raj, Amar
Larsson, Sylva
Patil, Ajay B.
Singh, Atul K.
Laajala, Mira
Kumpulainen, Tatu
Marjomäki, Varpu S.
Toppari, J. Jussi
coredata.dc:description: Here, we present two label-free ratiometric fluorosensors using CDs for selective and sensitive detection of enteroviral RNA. Using CDs (reference fluorophore), EB (specific fluorophore) and single-stranded DNA fragment (Probe), two fluorosensors were developed and compared in identifying target DNA sequence. Func sensor consists of CDs covalently functionalized with Probe and mixed with EB, while Non-Func sensor contains only a mixture of CDs, Probe and EB. In both sensors, the addition of the target DNA noticeably enhanced EB fluorescence, while CDs fluorescence changed slightly due to electron transfer between CDs and EB, thus enabling ratiometric detection. Non-Func sensor exhibited a sensitivity range of 100 nM-3 μM (Limit of Detection ∼88.7 nM) with the target DNA but no useable sensitivity range with real enteroviral RNA samples. In contrast, Func sensor showed a higher sensitivity range of 0.5 nM–3 μM (LOD ∼270 pM) with DNA and 0.3 nM–5 nM (LOD ∼67 pM) with viral RNA, as well as improved selectivity. The superiority of the Func sensor is likely owing to enhanced charge transfer between CDs and EB due to covalent functionalization. Furthermore, the Func sensor could detect in situ, and real-time the release of enteroviral RNA in vitro. Hence, we established a novel, sensitive, and selective method for detecting viral RNA, supported by a charge transfer mechanism between CDs and fluorophores.

Gratitude is also extended to the Central Research Facility (CRF) at IIT Delhi, India, for their exceptional XPS characterization services, and to the SAIF facilities at CUSAT University, Cochin, India, for providing HR-TEM morphological characterization results. Additional thanks goes to Mr. Santanu Kandar (Research scholar, Department of Physics, IIT Delhi) for the help in analyzing the XPS data. This work has been funded by Nanoscience Center (University of Jyv\u00E4skyl\u00E4, Finland) and Finnish Ministry of Education and Culture. Jane and Aatos Erkko foundation (#240002) and Research Council of Finland (#363656, #323995) are also greatly acknowledged for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) HR-TEM image of CDs showing particle size ranges from 1.53 to 5.02, SAED pattern of CDs showing diffused rings corresponding to the turbostratic graphite and Lattice fringes with d-spacing of 0.21 nm in highly amorphous CDs, B) Histogram analysis for the estimation of mean size of CDs particles with Gaussian fit C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs (black plot) with (λex)max ∼ 350 nm and PL spectra with (λem)max ∼ 440 nm. Red dashed line shows the Stoke's shift of ∼5983 cm−1. D) PL emission scan at different (λex) from 300 nm to 500 nm manifesting the small red shift at higher excitation wavelength E) FTIR spectrum of CDs and CDs-Probe to compare the surface functional groups before and after oligonucleotide functionalization F) Zeta potential analysis of CDs and CDs-Probe to compare the CDs surface potential before and after functionalization, as well as zeta potential of EB revealing positive charge in solution. Lower panels show DLS measurement of CDs and CDs-Probe to analyze the changes in hydrodynamic particle size. G) XRD pattern of carbon dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
XPS wide scan analysis with high-resolution C 1s and N 1s spectra of A-C) CDs, & D-F) CDs-Probe. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescence spectra of A) CDs C) CDs/Probe & E) CDs-Probe in different concentrations of EB (15 μM – 1920 μM). B, D & F) The linear regression plots of CDs, CDs/Probe & CDs-Probe were obtained between I0/I and EB concentration ranges from 15 μM – 1920 μM to carry out the Stern-Volmer calculations, error bars account for the deviation obtained from the replicates (n = 3) of IEB/ICDs based ratiometric fluorescence signals. The error was calculated using the formula: Δ(Io/I) = Io/I × ((ΔIo/Io)2+(ΔI/I)2)0.5. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Analytical performance of A) Func Sensor quantifying TS from 0.5 nM to 5 μM. D) Non-Func sensor quantifying TS from 0.5 nM to 3 μM. B) and E) are the zoomed plots of lowTS concentrations, i.e., vary from 0.5 nm to 50 nm. C) and F) are the linear regression plots of Func sensor & Non-Func sensor respectively. The error bars were calculated using the error propagation, Δ(I EB/I CD s) = I EB/I CD s × [(ΔI EB/I EB)2+(ΔI CD s/I CD s)2]½ , of standard deviations from 3 parallel datasets. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Selectivity assay to exhibit the selective sensing ability of A) Non-Func & B) Func sensors-in presence of complementary, mismatched and non-complementary oligonucleotide sequences (1 μM). The panels show the ratio of the relative changes in the intensities, i.e., (IEB/IEB,0)/(ICD s/ICD s ,0), where IEB,0 and ICD s ,0 are the control measurements for the EB and CDs fluorescence, respectively, taken before adding any of the target sequences. The error bars were calculated using the error propagation, Δ(Rel. IEB/Rel. ICD s) = Rel. IEB/Rel. ICD s × [(ΔIEB/IEB)2+(ΔICD s/ICD s)2+(ΔIEB,0/IEB,0)2+(ΔICD s ,0/ICD s ,0)2]½, of standard deviations from 3 parallel datasets. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

A) Real sample analysis using Func sensor to quantify several dilutions of enteroviral RNA with the decent range from 0.3 nM to 5 nM (Inset: Zoom in plot of EB and CDs response towards different concentrations of viral RNA). B) Linear regression plot showing linear trend of Func sensor against viral RNA concentrations (nM). The error bars were calculated using the error propagation, Δ(IEB/ICDs) = IEB/ICDs × [(ΔIEB/IEB)2+(ΔICDs/ICDs)2]½, of standard deviations from 3 parallel datasets. C) DLS measurement showing size distribution of enterovirus particles after treatment in NK buffer at + 37 °C up until 200 min (from grey to brown curve) following temperature increase to + 50 °C in the last two timepoints 240 and 360 min, olive green and orange curves, respectively. D) PL intensity of the Func sensor normalized to CDs-probe intensity, in the presence of enterovirus particles after treatment in NK buffer as described in C. E) Zoom in plot of EB response in D. F) Ratiometric fluorescence response of Func sensor calculated from D. G) Representative scheme of sensing viral RNA with Func sensor in the presence of Echovirus 1. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Schematic pictorial illustration (not in scale) of fabrication and operation principle of CDs and EB based Non-Func sensor (CDs/Probe/EB) and Func sensor (CDs-Probe/EB) for the ultrasensitive detection of enterovirus RNA. Spectra below show representative fluorescence measurement of the system within the picture together with a photograph of the fluorescence excited with UV light. The spectra are normalized to the fluorescence of CD at 440 nm. In Non-Func sensor the CDs absorbing at 350 nm and fluorescing at 440 nm are mixed with the Probe DNA and EB, which induces the partial fluorescence quenching of CDs and slight EB-fluorescence at 640 nm. In the presence of target sequence (TS) the fluorescence of CDs if partially recovered and EB-fluorescence is clearly enhanced. Func sensor comprised of CDs with covalently immobilized probe. Its spectral properties still show absorption at 350 nm and emission at 440 nm. When mixed with EB, it also exhibits quenching of CDs-fluorescence, which is recovered in presence of TS. However, the EB-fluorescence exhibits a vast enhancement in presence of TS.
List of functional groups and their respective binding energies in the deconvoluted XPS spectrum.

List of sequences to test the selectivity of Non - Func & Func sensors. The last 16 bases of the target sequence hybridize with the probe AC CCA CAG GCA CAA AG--AAA–NH2–5’. In other sequences the mismatches are marked with bold.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we present two label-free ratiometric fluorosensors using CDs for selective and sensitive detection of enteroviral RNA. Using CDs (reference fluorophore), EB (specific fluorophore) and single-stranded DNA fragment (Probe), two fluorosensors were developed and compared in identifying target DNA sequence. Func sensor consists of CDs covalently functionalized with Probe and mixed with EB, while Non-Func sensor contains only a mixture of CDs, Probe and EB. In both sensors, the addition of the target DNA noticeably enhanced EB fluorescence, while CDs fluorescence changed slightly due to electron transfer between CDs and EB, thus enabling ratiometric detection. Non-Func sensor exhibited a sensitivity range of 100 nM-3 μM (Limit of Detection ∼88.7 nM) with the target DNA but no useable sensitivity range with real enteroviral RNA samples. In contrast, Func sensor showed a higher sensitivity range of 0.5 nM–3 μM (LOD ∼270 pM) with DNA and 0.3 nM–5 nM (LOD ∼67 pM) with viral RNA, as well as improved selectivity. The superiority of the Func sensor is likely owing to enhanced charge transfer between CDs and EB due to covalent functionalization. Furthermore, the Func sensor could detect in situ, and real-time the release of enteroviral RNA in vitro. Hence, we established a novel, sensitive, and selective method for detecting viral RNA, supported by a charge transfer mechanism between CDs and fluorophores.

Carbon dots (CDs) and Ethidium Bromide (EB) based two ratiometric fluorosensors: a Non-Func sensor (CDs/Probe/EB) and a Func sensor (CDs-Probe/EB) for the ultrasensitive detection of enterovirus RNA.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Enterovirus (EV) commonly causes mild feverish illness in younger ones; however, it can also lead to serious conditions such as meningoencephalitis, myelitis, myocarditis, sepsis-like syndrome, respiratory problems, and acute hepatitis [1]. Some EV types, like EV-A71 and EV-D68, are linked to outbreaks with significant morbidity and mortality [2,3]. Early and accurate detection, along with genetic characterization of EVs in properly collected samples, is needed at both individual and community levels [4]. Swift laboratory diagnosis can limit unnecessary investigations, shorten hospital stays, and lower the risk of complications [1,4]. Furthermore, as enteroviruses still lack clinically proven antivirals for therapy, novel methods are needed, e.g., to detect the RNA release from the virions, which is a necessary step for productive infection, and which is also a good target for future antivirals [5]. The conventional enterovirus detection includes virus culture, molecular techniques such as reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) and serological methods like ELISA and immunoassays. Despite the good sensitivity, many of the conventional methods are time-consuming procedures, expensive and laborious, and do not facilitate real-time monitoring of RNA release [6,7]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop viral sensor platforms that are robust, cost-effective, and capable of providing rapid, real-time detection of viral RNA. Such a platform would facilitate in situ detection of virus activation, thereby enabling even new ways in finding novel antivirals and advancing fundamental research on infectious entry pathways for healthcare applications.

Over a few decades fluorescence nanoparticles (FNP) have significantly manifested the robust and precise optical sensing of bioanalytes such as viruses [8–10]. Of late, carbon dots (CDs), zero-dimensional FNP, have emerged as a promising tool with exciting biological applications such as bio-sensing, medical imaging, and drug delivery [11–13]. As compared to the contemporary FNPs, CDs superiority is based on its streamline synthesis, high photostability, tunability of photoluminescence (PL), good aqueous solubility, low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility, and abundance of surface functionalities for ligand conjugation such as nucleic acids and proteins [14–19]. The emergence of CDs is most suited for biological applications, and has been significantly used in sensing biomarkers, proteins and pathogens, predominantly [20–26]. Moreover, the feasibility of CDs to operate as energy donors or acceptors and convert the corresponding optical changes to measurable signals such as fluorescence intensity has been significantly investigated in virus biosensing [27,28]. To achieve early detection of SARS-CoV2 RNA, Sheffield et al., reported complementary antisense oligonucleotide conjugated CDs to facilitate aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) of the measured fluorescence after hybridization with SARS-CoV-2 RNA [29]. Furthermore, carbon nanoparticles along with two-probe fluorescent silver nanocluster (AgNC) were used as the fluorescence acceptor and donor pair in the development of a Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) free biosensor for HIV detection. In another interesting report, when the target was not present CDs probe was adsorbed on the Fe3O4@Au surface which induced fluorescence quenching. The CDs fluorescence was recovered in the presence of the target DNA forming a double stranded (ds) DNA with the probe, thus preventing the adsorption of CDs on the Fe3O4@Au. This strategy was adopted for the detection of Human T-lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1) [30].

The drawback of the current CDs based fluorescence probes is that they many times rely on strategies based on emission intensity of one type of molecules, such as fluorescence enhancement or quenching, which are prone to the errors due to background noise, instrumental factors, matrix effects, and variations in the concentration [31,32]. Therefore, to address this problem, ratiometric fluorescence techniques are gaining attention for their ability to eliminate environmental effects and provide more precise measurements [33,34]. These systems typically consist of two materials with different fluorescence emission wavelengths: one serves as an invariant reference signal, while the other acts as the sensing signal [35,36]. By measuring the ratio of the two fluorescent peaks instead of the absolute intensity of one, this approach minimizes background interference and enhances sensitivity and accuracy [37]. Thus CD-based ratiometric fluorosensors have become more common in recent times and used as crucial fluorophores for various applications. For instance, blue emissive CDs with red-emissive Glutathione (GSH)-CdTe quantum dots (QDs) based ratiometric fluorosensor was constructed for detecting NO2 [38]. Another CDs-AuNC-based ratiometric fluorosensor was developed for cysteine sensing by estimating FRET [39]. In this context, a ratiometric fluorescent CDs detection system has been developed for point-of-care testing (POCT) of Cys C, utilizing FRET and the specific inhibitory effect of Cys C on papain [40]. Furthermore, a simple strategy for the specific detection of ClO− was manifested using CDs and Ru[(bpy)3]2+ nanocomposite based ratiometric sensing platform [41]. Similarly, several CDs-based ratiometric sensing platforms have been designed for heavy metals detection such as Hg2+ [42], cellular Cu2+, or superoxide anion [43–45]. Moreover, ratiometric fluorosensors for the detection of nucleic acid have also been reported in different studies. A related CDs and 3-mercapropionic acid-coated CdTe QD-based ratiometric fluorosensor was constructed for the detection of HIV dsDNA [46]. A distinctive CDs-ethidium bromide (EB) based ratiometric nanosensor was also demonstrated for the selective detection of dsDNA with broad dynamic linear range of 1.0 mM–100 mM, and low detection limit down to 0.47 mM [47]. However, sensitivity and selectivity are still the biggest challenge in constructing a robust virus biosensing platform with precise and short-turn-around time of detection [22,23,42–46].

Hence, inspired by these research studies, we present here two variants of CDs (blue emissive)/EB (red emissive)-based ratiometric fluorosensor for label-free virus RNA biosensing, as shown in Scheme 1 . EB was chosen as the selective fluorophore due to its well reported properties, in particular, its efficient intercalation capability on dsDNA or dsRNA which enhances its fluorescence and thus enables visual detection of nucleic acids in many everyday assays, like gel electrophoresis [48–50]. The first variant is based on the straightforward mixing of CDs, specific 19-base-long oligonucleotide (probe, for the sequence see section 2.2.3) and EB (CDs/Probe/EB) like the previously reported sensor [47]. This sensor is called Non-Func hereafter. Furthermore, to improve the performance of this sensor, the second variant involves covalent bonding between the probe oligonucleotide and CDs. In other words, the CDs is covalently functionalized with the probe before it is mixed with EB. This sensor is called Func sensor, and it yields much better sensitivity and selectivity of target enteroviral RNA even at the lower concentrations. On the other hand, Non-Func sensor showed a limited selectivity and sensitivity to the enteroviral RNA. The performances of the sensors were first validated by in vitro studies using a target DNA sequence (TS) resembling 32 bases long conserved region of enterovirus RNA (For the sequence see Table 2). Further validation of the sensors was carried out by using extracted enteroviral RNA, and in the case of the Func sensor, also by an assay where the enterovirus particle was allowed to open and release the RNA in real-time in vitro. These tests provided clear comparison between the sensors, which were supported by fluorescence lifetime studies and mechanistic insights.

Based on these criteria, we have compared our sensor with recent developments in nucleic acid detecting ratiometric fluorosensors based on carbon dots [25,26,46,47,51–53]. The comparative analysis summarized in supplementary information (SI) Table S6, shows that our sensor performs well among the CDs-based sensors in general and is among the best ones for full viral RNA detection. All the most sensitive ratiometric sensors are aimed to detect short nucleic acid strands, which easily bind to the complementary probe DNA/RNA. However, the full enteroviral RNA is ∼7500 bases long which induces vast amounts of steric hindrance on hybridization and easily causes long distance between the hybridized part and the CD in Non-Func sensor. The bonding between the CD and the Probe in Func sensor circumvents also this problem.

Our proof-of-principle study highlights the importance of covalent immobilization of the probe for improved electron transfer between CDs and EB and thus enhanced performance and demonstrate the suitability of the Func sensor for practical applications in rapid, real-time and precise in situ detection of viral RNA in vitro. Especially, we show that the Func sensor can be used as a novel viral RNA sensing platform offering the much-needed possibility for real-time detection of virus activation during infection. However, for future in vivo and clinical applications, less cytotoxic probes are needed.

Citric acid (C6H8O7) and ethylenediamine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Quinine sulfate was used as a fluorescence quantum yield standard to calculate quantum yield and was purchased from VWR Pvt. Ltd. The pH of CDs solution was adjusted using 1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) solutions. Highly purified Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ cm at 25 °C) was used as a solvent for the experimental investigations. The fluorescence studies were carried out in black 96-well microplates formerly called as Cell-Carrier Ultra microplates and were purchased from Revvity Health Science Pvt. Ltd. (formerly called as PerkinElmer). The dialysis membrane used for the CDs purification was purchased from Spectra/Por with the pore size 0.5–1 Kda.

A549 cell line (adenocarcinoma human alveolar basal epithelial cells) was obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (Manassas, VA, USA) and was cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM). The culture media was supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1 % penicillin−streptomycin, and 1 % GlutaMAX, all from Gibco (Paisley, UK). The cell culture was maintained in a humidified incubator with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave assisted hydrothermal synthesis of CD
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis method has been described in supplementary material (SI) section S1.

The UV–Vis absorption studies were carried out on PerkinElmer Lambda 650 UV–Vis spectrometer using a quartz cuvette with 10 mm optical pathlength. Fluorescence measurements were carried out on Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer with a similar cuvette. Fluorescent decay profiles were measured with the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) setup based on the HydraHarp 400 (PicoQuant) TCSCP module. The excitation source consisted of a PicoQuant PDL 800-D pulsed diode laser driver and a diode laser head (LDH-PC-375) emitting light at 375 nm wavelength. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurements were carried out using a Nicolet Nexus 670 FT-IR. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and zetapotential measurements were carried out with the Malvern Zetasizer Ultra ZS90 instrument (Malvern Instruments Ltd., United Kingdom). A Kratos Analytical, AXIS Supra is employed to perform X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to analyze the surface chemical composition and changes in the chemical environment and tracking the shifts in the binding energies. CasaXPS software is used for peak fitting functions and background subtraction. The operational parameters included a monochromatic Al Kα source (1486.6 eV). The morphological analysis was carried out using a JEOL 2100 HR-TEM machine and imaged by Gatan UltraScan 2k × 2k CCD camera. All the measurement were carried out in Milli-Q water at standard conditions. The technical information of the FTIR, DLS, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Zeta potential techniques have been explained in SI S11.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface functionalization of single stranded oligonucleotides to CDs surface
ce:section.ce:para: A linking chemistry based-on 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)-propyl)carbodiimide (EDC, 1.0 mM) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 1.0 mM) was utilized to functionalize 5′ amino flanked single stranded oligonucleotide (Probe, 100 μM) having a sequence 5′–NH2–AAA-GA AAC ACG GAC ACC CA to CDs (∼0.45 mg/mL), respectively. The last 16 bases of the Probe have been designed to the unique target enteroviral RNA conserved 16 base sequences as mentioned in Table 2. The three Adenine next to the amino group act only as spacer.The amide bond formation takes place between primary amino group of nucleotides and activated carboxylic groups of CDs. The recipe of the reaction master mix is as follows:
50 μL CDs (0.45 mg/mL) + 250 μL EDC (100 mM) + 250 μL NHS (100 mM) + 50 μL Probe (100 μM) + 800 μL MilliQ water.

The reaction master mix was prepared and gently stirred at 25 °C. After a 15-h incubation, the solution was used for dialysis purification using 0.5–1.0 KDa dialysis membrane. UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy was then performed on the purified functionalized CDs solution. The preparation details of Non-Func and Func sensors have been discussed in SI S11.

The CDs and CDs-Probe (originally prepared in MilliQ) were prepared in DMEM media with concentrations ranges from 0.1 μM to 500 μM (for CDs) and 0.45 μM–2250 μM (for CDs-Probe) to check their cytotoxicity effect on the live A549 mammalian cell lines. The preparation details of CDs and CDs-Probe incubated with A549 cells have been discussed in SI S10.

Both sensors were tested against real enteroviral RNA. The viral RNA from Echovirus 1 (Farouk strain, ATCC) was isolated using High Pure viral RNA kit (Roche), according to the instructions of the manufacturer. The concentration of freshly isolated enteroviral RNA was analyzed using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and measured as 65 ng/μL corresponding to 13.3 nM stock concentration. To simplify the several dilutions of enteroviral RNA, the stock solution was tuned to 10 nM.

For the experiment with Func sensor where real virus opens and releases the RNA the CDs-Probe (0.32 μg/ml), EB (60 μg/ml) and 4 μg (5.23 × 109 PFU) of enterovirus (Echovirus 1, Farouk strain, ATCC) were prepared in NK-buffer (20 mM NaCl, 6 mM KH2PO4, 12 mM K2HPO4, pH 7.25) and incubated at 37 °C for 200 min. Finally, the temperature was increased to 50 °C for the last 160 min. At each timepoint, the sample was analyzed using both DLS and the ratiometric fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and working principle of Non-Func and Func ratiometric sensor systems
ce:section.ce:para: We designed ratiometric sensors based on CDs and Ethidium bromide (CDs/EB) dual fluorophore to quantify enteroviral RNA. The first sensor, having straightforward mixing of CDs, Probe, i.e., 19-bases-long oligonucleotide with 16 subsequent bases complementary to the conserved region of the enterovirus RNA, and EB (CDs/Probe/EB), is called Non-Func sensor, and its fabrication and operation principle are schematically shown in Scheme 1 (Top). The second sensor includes Probe-functionalized-CDs with a covalent linking, mixed with EB and is known as Func sensor. It is presented in Scheme 1 (Bottom).

Citric acid and ethylenediamine (EDA) based bright blue emissive CDs were synthesized using the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method (SI, section S1) with slight modification from reaction stoichiometry mentioned in the previously published literature [54]. The as-prepared CDs dispersion remains clear and homogeneous at room temperature over a few months. Fig. 1 A shows the High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) micrograph of CDs, which was drop-cast from the water dispersion on a carbon-coated copper grid. HR-TEM micrographs demonstrate the morphological structures of standard CDs morphologies with uniform particle diameter of about 3.0 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. 1B). The zoomed HR-TEM micrograph in Fig. 1A (lower right) shows amorphous CD with a lattice stripe (d-spacing) of 0.21 nm within the fringes with face spacing consistent with the (100) face spacing of graphite [55]. This was complemented by the diffused rings appearing in the Selective Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern as shown in Fig. 1A (Upper right). The histogram analysis (Fig. 1B) exhibits the different CDs particle size ranges from 1.51 nm to 5.02 nm with an average size of 3.03 nm using Gaussian fit.

The absorption spectrum of CDs showed absorbance in the UV and visible wavelengths having two unique absorption bands at 245 nm and 350 nm, respectively. The absorption band at 245 nm corresponds to π–π∗ transitions in the aromatic functional groups such as CC and CN and has a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 30 nm. The second absorption band at 350 nm is attributed to n–π∗ transitions due to surface functional groups such as carbonyl groups (CO) having lone pair of electrons as shown in Fig. S1. The Stokes shift between maximum excitation and emission peaks of CDs was measured as 5983 cm−1 (Fig. 1C).

Moreover, the synthesized CDs were functionalized with the 19-base-long single-stranded oligonucleotide DNA sequence (Probe) by using a well-known amide surface functionalization chemistry (see materials and methods). The strong absorbance band of Probe after functionalization with the CDs surface (CDs-Probe) was clearly seen at 256 nm with CDs peak at 347 nm as shown in Fig. S1 confirming the functionalization of Probe on CDs surface. The absorbance band of Probe in the non-functionalized CDs (CDs/Probe) was not visible around 260 nm and only the π–π∗ transition peak of CDs at 245 nm was evident.

Fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs was observed at (λex)max ∼ 350 nm for which the corresponding highest emission peak was observed at (λem)max ∼ 440 nm. The color of the diluted CDs solution was pale yellow when observed under bright light, however, a bright blue fluorescence was evident when excited with a UV lamp, as shown in Fig. 1C inset. The quantum yield of CDs was calculated using absorption optical density data and integrated fluorescence intensities of a standard (Quinine sulfate) and the CDs. The calculated quantum yield (SI, section S2) of the synthesized CDs was as high as ∼69 % and was optimized by calibrating the synthesis parameter as demonstrated in SI (Table S1). The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was investigated at different excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. 1D and found to be the same. In other words, CDs show excitation-independent PL where the maximum emission remains at the same wavelength and the intensity follows the absorption of the excitation wavelength, as evident from the 3D contour map presented in the inset of Fig. 1D. Furthermore, PL emission peak (Fig. S6 and Table S3) of CDs-Probe (Functionalized CDs) showed quenching and a tiny blue shift as compared to the CDs/Probe (Non-Functionalized CDs) which again complement the confirmation of CDs functionalization with the Probe strand [15].

The two most popular models to understand PL emission mechanism of carbon dots are based on creation of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and surface defects. The band gap transitions in the internal π region due to radiative emission may be the source of photoluminescence in CDs and it often referred as “carbon core state” emission. The surface defect states are usually generated on the shell region of CDs and often referred to as “spherical shells” around the carbon core. It has been hypothesized that these spherical shells mostly consist of dangling bonds including oxygen-containing surface functionalities. The dangling bond surface defects originate due to the gradual surface oxidation of CDs. These surface defects result in the regulation of CDs PL emission by functioning as capture centers for emissive excitons. The higher the degree of surface oxidation in CDs, the greater the number of surface defects that resulted in higher emissive sites [14,56].

FTIR spectrum for the CDs (Fig. 1E) has broad but weak peaks with maximum at ∼3500 cm−1 and 3300 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (-OH) and primary amines (-NH2) functional groups. The weak peak at 2800 cm−1 attributes to alkane (C–H). The sharp peak at 1700 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of carboxylic group (CO). The bending peaks of secondary amine (-NH2) was evident at 1650 cm−1.

FTIR results showed the availability of hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs which explain its excellent water solubility and stability in aqueous system. FTIR spectrum for the functionalized CDs (CDs-Probe) (Fig. 1E) demonstrated the presence of similar functional groups as in free CDs with slight frequency shifts at 1710 cm−1, 1640 cm−1 and 1190 cm−1. The observed similarity arises because the incorporation of the probe into CDs does not result in the loss of functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH) and amine (-NH2). This is due to the probe itself exhibiting these characteristic peaks in the FTIR spectra. However, CDs-Probe IR exhibits peak at ∼1225 cm−1 that attributes to the stretching vibration of bonded phosphorous oxide (P–O). This strongly suggest that the single stranded oligonucleotide has been functionalized on the CDs surface while restoring the surface functionalities of bare CDs.

The zeta (ζ) potentials of CDs and CDs-Probe were measured to illustrate and confirm the functionalization of the CDs surface with ssDNA by analyzing the surface charge change as exhibited in Fig. 1F. The short ssDNA fragment is negatively charged due to the presence of phosphate groups. The charge over the free CDs surface was measured to be negative ∼ −5.5 mV and could be due to equal surface charge distribution from carboxylic and primary/secondary amines. However, after the surface functionalization of CDs with the probe oligonucleotide, there was a noticeable change in the surface charge of functionalized CDs (∼−29 mV). The remarkable change in surface charge density suggests the comprehensive attachment of ssDNA fragment over the CDs surface. The ζ of EB was measured to be +6.3 mV, consistent with the fact that EB is a positively charged molecule. This positive ζ will facilitate the stability of the complex system containing CDs with a negative ζ. DLS studies indicated that CDs average hydrodynamic diameters were ∼8.3. DLS measurements performed on CDs-Probe revealed a significant increase in their hydrodynamic diameter (∼43.73 nm) compared to CDs, without any noticeable changes in their PDI values.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of CDs was obtained by drop-casting the sample onto a silica wafer and drying it at 60 °C for 24 h. The spectrum (Fig. 1G) shows two peaks at ∼23.5° and ∼43.2°, corresponding to d-spacings of 0.36 nm and 0.23 nm, respectively. The peak at 23.5° indicates a slightly larger d-spacing than the 002 plane in graphite, suggesting a non-ideal graphite-like structure. The peak at 43.2° is also observed in some graphite samples, indicating the presence of a 100 plane [57,58]. The XRD pattern thus suggest the amorphous nature of the synthesized CDs.

The chemical composition analysis was carried out using X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The wide scan XPS spectrum of CDs attributed the presence of C, O and N with atomic percentages of 70.4 %, 10.7 %, and 19.0 %, respectively (Fig. 2 A). The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum revealed that there are three different types of carbon atoms in CDs, including graphitic and aliphatic (CC and C–C), N-containing (C–N, CN), and oxygenated (C–O and CO) (Fig. 2B).

The deconvoluted N 1s spectrum correlates with C 1s spectrum and exhibits the presences of C–N–C and N-(C)3 functional groups, as shown in Fig. 2C. The XPS spectrum of CDs clearly demonstrated the nitrogen doping within its carbon core. See the list of functional groups and their binding energies in Table 1 .

The functionalization of CDs, i.e., the attachment of Probe over CDs surface, was carried out using EDC/NHS-based click chemistry to induce robust peptide covalent bond for strong attachment of the ssDNA over CDs surface. To confirm the functionalization of CDs, XPS was carried out also for the functionalized CDs (CDs-Probe) to compare the available functional groups. The wide scan XPS spectrum of CDs-Probe attributes the presence of C, O and N with atomic percentages of 67.4 %, 8.2 %, and 24.4 %, respectively (Fig. 2D). The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum manifested similar functional groups as in case of free CDs as shown in Fig. 2E. The interesting observation in C 1s deconvoluted spectrum of CDs-Probe is that the relative intensity of C–N peak has been elevated as compared to the one for CDs. Moreover, the relative intensity of N-(C)3 peak in the deconvoluted N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2F) is evidently higher in CDs-Probe than in plain CDs which reflects the formation of peptide bond after functionalizing the amino group of Probe with CD's carboxylic group.

Before studying the response of the full sensors on the target DNA/RNA, the fluorescence response of Non-Func and Func sensor was comprehensively evaluated while combining CDs, probe and EB. This was done by deciphering the fluorescence quenching rate of CDs in presence of EB. The specific concentration of CDs (0.45 mg/mL) was prepared and mixed with 30 μM EB solution. The mixing of EB with CDs solution resulted in its adsorption on CDs surface causing fluorescence quenching.

It was observed that the CDs fluorescence always quenched within less than 2 min of contact time with EB and was steady thereafter during the analysis time. Therefore, to be on the safe side, detection time for all the readings were documented after 5 min from mixing.

To validate the quenching mechanism between CDs and EB, the concentration of EB was increased gradually from 0 to 1920 μM. With increasing concentration of EB, the emission peak position of CDs manifested blue-shift from 448 nm to 431 nm which implies surface interaction between CDs and EB. The gradual CDs fluorescence quenching rate with increase in EB concentration could be tentatively attributed to the photoinduced electron transfer between the surface functional groups of CDs and EB. The fluorescence quenching of CDs in presence of EB can also be explained by the Stern–Volmer equation:
(1)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K sv
[ Q ]
where I 0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of EB, respectively. [Q] is the concentration of EB and K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, respectively. Fig. 3 presents a typical Stern–Volmer plot for three combinations namely, CDs/EB (Fig. 3A), CDs/Probe/EB (simple mixing of CDs, probe and EB, Fig. 3C) and CDs-Probe/EB (Mixing of probe-functionalized CDs with EB, Fig. 3E). The trend in all the three combinations projects the systematic quenching with increase in EB concentration from 0 to 1920 μM having decent linearity between I 0/I and EB concentrations as shown in Figs. 3B,D,F. The slopes from the linear fits were i) 2.7 × 10−3 μM−1 for CDs/EB with correlation coefficient of 0.992, ii) 3.0 × 10−3 μM−1 for CDs/Probe/EB with correlation coefficient of 0.996, and iii) 4.0 × 10−3 μM−1 for CDs-Probe/EB with correlation coefficient of 0.991.

The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using standard equation:
(2)
LOD =
3 σ
s
,
where σ is the standard deviation of five measurements from control samples such as CDs, CDs/Probe and CDs-Probe and s is the slope of the calibration curve used to calculate the quenching constant K sv in all the three combinations. LODs of the combinations with EB were calculated as i) 0.86 μM, ii) 0.78 μM and iii) 0.31 μM.

The stability tests were performed with similar experimental optimization to study the fluorescence response of CDs, EB, CDs-Probe, and CDs/EB under varying pH, ionic concentration, reaction time, temperature, and continuous UV-light irradiation. The pH of CDs/EB, CDs/Probe/EB, CDs-Probe/EB and CDs-Probe/EB/Target Sequence (TS) solutions was varied from 4 to 11, with no drastic change in the fluorescence intensity but only a weak local maximum around pH 6–7 (Fig. S2). Stability tests under continuous UV-light irradiation, shown in SI in Fig. S3, revealed steady fluorescence with intensities remaining stable for at least 60 min for all the solutions. Only a minimal decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed after 100 min of continuous UV irradiation. On the other hand, temperature profiling, shown in SI in Fig. S4, indicated no significant variation in fluorescence emission intensity, demonstrating the stability of both CDs variants for use in varying biological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic consideration: Electron-transfer based fluorescence quenching of CDs in presence of EB
ce:section.ce:para: This section discusses the mechanistic insights behind the CDs and EB fluorophore-based ratiometric fluorosensors. The quantum yield of the optimized CDs was calculated to be ∼69 % (Section S2 of ESI). These bright blue CDs, when mixed with EB and followed by the addition of a target sequence, exhibited a notable reduction in CDs fluorescence intensity and a significant enhancement in EB fluorescence intensity. The ratio of both fluorescence intensities was used for the quantification of enteroviral RNA.

To understand the ratiometric response of CDs and EB, both electron transfer (ET) and FRET were considered as plausible quenching mechanisms [47,59]. The interaction between CDs and EB was investigated to decipher the possibilities of ET or FRET-based quenching. Fig. 4 A and B shows the fluorescence and absorbance of CDs (2.8 μg/ml), EB (96 μM), and their mixture at identical concentrations. The absorption of the mixture is the sum of the individual components, as seen in Fig. 4B. Fluorescence spectra were analyzed using log-normal band shape functions (SI section S4). The fluorescence of EB only and EB in CDs/EB mixture shows no change in the area under the curve (AUC ∼ 19000), full width half maxima (FWHM ∼ 123 nm), and (λem)max ∼ 636 nm. This lack of change ruled out the possibility of FRET based quenching between CDs and EB, as significant fluorescence enhancement of EB would be expected in the case of FRET [60].

However, comparing CDs only and CDs in CDs/EB mixture shows the quenching of CDs fluorescence in CDs/EB, with AUC decreasing by ∼59 %, FWHM decreasing by 11 nm, and a blue shift of 11 nm in (λem)max, as shown in Table S2 and Fig. S5. The quenching can be attributed to electron transfer from excited CDs to EB, reducing CDs fluorescence. The distinct narrowing and a blueshift of the CD's fluorescence band additionally indicates that the red part of the spectrum is quenched more efficiently. This suggests that the low-energy side contains a larger contribution from surface states that are more accessible to EB. Lifetime decay analysis shows only a slight decrease in CD's average decay time from 14.84 ns to 13.82 ns upon addition of EB. This slight decrease in lifetime is most likely associated with dynamic (collisional) quenching. However, the reduction in the steady-state fluorescence intensity is significantly larger than the reduction in the lifetime demonstrating that majority of the quenching is due to static quenching with a quenching rate beyond the time-resolution of our TCSPC instrument [61].

Fluorescence properties of CDs/Probe and CDs-Probe were examined using lifetime decay profiles and spectral analysis. The spectral analysis showed an identical emission peak maxima at 441.5 nm but lower intensity and a slight spectral broadening in CDs-Probe (shown in Table S3, Fig. S6), indicating a slight modification in the structure owing to the condensation reaction between surface functional group and probe. Consistent with the fluorescent emission quenching, CDs-Probe exhibited similar decay dynamics, with an average lifetime decreasing from 14.8 ns to 12.4 ns. From Table S5, the quantum yield of CDs also doesn't seem to change upon functionalization with probe. Upon addition of EB in Func and Non-Func systems, significant changes in the CD's fluorescence emission spectra were observed, as shown in Fig. 4C, with the corresponding absorbance profile in Fig. 4D. The Func CDs shows ∼90 % quenching, with the area under the curve decreasing from 1040 in CDs/Probe/EB to ∼100 in CDs-Probe/EB. Other fluorescence spectral parameters (peak center, FWHM, skewness) remain the same for both cases, as shown in Fig. S9. Fluorescence decay analysis also reveals that the lifetime is shorter for CDs in the CDs-Probe/EB case (11.5 ns), while the lifetime of CDs in CDs/Probe/EB remains unchanged at 14.8 ns, similar to bare CDs (14.8 ns) and CDs/Probe (14.8 ns) systems. The observed fluorescence and lifetime decrement upon adding EB is due to EB forming semi-intercalating complexes with ss-DNA, enhancing the interaction between CDs and EB and resulting in quenching behavior [48,49].

Introducing a target sequence to sensors containing EB increases the EB fluorescence due to intercalation. Fig. 4E and S10 clearly show that the EB band in both sensors have the same spectral parameters and completely overlaps, indicating that surface functionalization does not hinder intercalation. EB's phenyl and ethyl groups interact with the DNA helix (∼5 EB molecules per sequence) [49], enhancing fluorescence. Intercalation causes a red shift in EB absorbance and blueshifts fluorescence from 636 nm to 615 nm (Fig. 4A and E), indicating changes in the local environment upon intercalation [62,63].

Spectral analysis of CDs fluorescence in the presence of the target sequence DNA shows approximately 90 % more quenching in the Func sensor (Fig. 4E), similar to what was observed in the CDs-Probe/EB case (Fig. 4C). The overall CDs concentration is significantly low, making it difficult to observe quenching in the presence of the target sequence individually. Therefore, to understand the effect of the target sequence on the CDs, we analyzed the decay profile. The decay profile (from Fig. 4G and I, and Table S4) shows that fluorescence is significantly quenched, with the lifetime of CDs decreasing to 9.3 ns in CDs-Probe/EB/TS, while it remains 14.8 ns in CDs/Probe/EB/TS. Additionally, the IRF-limited ultrafast decay component was observed, and the decay components are tabulated in Table S4. In the case of CDs-Probe/EB and CDs-Probe/EB/TS, the ultrafast component of ∼20 ps with positive amplitude was observed, which corresponds to the electron transfer rate, while in the rest of the cases, 70–110 ps with a negative amplitude was observed, similar to the CDs decay profile (shown in Fig. 4H–J).

The decay component of ∼20 ps in the Func sensor confirms the improved ET, thus improved quenching of CDs and better ratiometric quantification. The difference between the sensors lies in the close proximity of EB due to the semi and full intercalating complexes formed by covalently CDs-bonded ss-DNA (Probe) and full intercalating complexes (hybridized TS) in the Func sensor. In contrast, in the Non-Func sensor, the intercalated complexes move freely, leaving the CD's fluorescence signal unaltered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of N on- F unc and Func sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Similarly, the Func sensor, consisting of similar concentrations of CDs functionalized with Probe and EB was screened with different concentrations of TS ranging from 0.5 nM to 5 μM. As shown in Fig. 5A–C, increasing target concentrations led to a gradual increase in EB's PL emission intensity, while CD's intensity remained unaffected. A linear relationship was observed from 0.5 nM to 5 μM, with a regression equation, y = 7.88 × 10−4 (nM−1) ∗x + 0.27 and an R2 value of 0.980. LOD was 270 pM, with limited standard error bars (Fig. 5C). The calculation was carried out in the same way as in case of Non-Func sensor. The control parameters of the Func sensor component, i.e., CDs-Probe, was also investigated (SI, section S9, Fig. S14) with different concentrations of TS and no significant trend was observed ruling out the possibility of direct role of the bare CDs-Probe in the analytical performance of Func sensor. Additionally, in Fig. 5B and E, the performance of the Func and Non-Func sensors with low TS concentrations can be compared. The Func sensor shows a relatively significant change even at lower concentrations of TS, which is useful information in terms of applicability with real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating selectivity of N on- F unc and Func sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of Non-Func and Func sensors was evaluated by investigating and comparing their PL emission response in presence of target sequence resembling the enteroviral RNA, TS having 3 to 6 mismatched bases or fully non-complementary sequence. Here the sequences opted were of 32 bases with the last 16 bases hybridizing with the probe. Table 2 shows the list of sequences used to analyze the selectivity of both the sensors.

The blank, consisting of only CDs and EB, was measured to estimate the relative PL emission intensity of Non-Func and Func sensors. The relative ratiometric fluorescence response (IEB/ICDs) was investigated for the various sequences mentioned in Table 2. IEB/ICDs observed for the Non-Func sensor (Fig. 6 A) showed minimal variation across different types of sequences. Specifically, the sensor's response was almost identical for complementary and non-complementary sequences, and mismatched sequences. This lack of significant change in IEB/ICDs based fluorescence signals implied the limited selectivity of Non-Func sensor. The experimental evidence demonstrates that all sequences induce a substantial ratiometric response as shown in Fig. 6A but there is no selectivity. This suggests that in all the sequences, the fluorescence response of intercalated EB is dominant, overshadowing the fluorescence signals from CDs. Consequently, the ratiometric response fails to demonstrate selectivity among complementary, non-complementary, and mismatched sequences in case of the Non-Func sensor.

On the other hand, Func sensor exhibited high selectivity with the IEB/ICDs based ratiometric fluorescence response at 350 nm excitation being higher for complementary and decreasing gradually from 3-mismatch to 4-mismatch to 6-mismatch and to finally non-complementary sequences as shown in Fig. 6B. Func sensor consists of single stranded oligonucleotide functionalized CDs mixed with fixed concentration of EB.

As a well-known fact, the higher the hybridization and intercalation of EB, the greater the EB fluorescence intensity. Since in Func sensor the probe is functionalized on the CDs surface, the intercalated EB remains in the proximity of CDs-Probe and has thus stronger interaction with the CDs. This may enhance the changes in the EB emission leading to better ratiometric fluorescence response, thus resulting in Func sensor being a significant selective sensor. On the other hand, the probe being attached from one end to the CDs might lower the hybridization affinity of other sequences compared to freely available probes. This might also result in some enhanced selectivity of Func sensor compared to Non-Func. However, the above time-resolved studies of CDs-Probe and CDs/Probe with EB suggest in the favor of the former reasoning.

It must be noted that the impact of the CDs fluorescence has not been detected and hence, CDs could only act as internal standard fluorophore. The fluorescence response of only EB intercalated with hybridized DNA did not show the linear response due to which the sensitivity and selectivity was extremely low. It is because CDs play the role of internal standard, the linearity of the ratiometric fluorosensor improves significantly resulting in the high sensitivity and selectivity of the sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of N on- F unc and Func sensors to detect full enteroviral RNA
ce:section.ce:para: IEB/ICDs based ratiometric fluorescence response of optimized Non-Func and Func sensors were investigated in presence of enteroviral RNA extracted from real enteroviruses. The dilutions of enteroviral RNA includes 0.3 nM, 0.5 nM, 1 nM, 2 nM and 5 nM. This range of concentrations turned out to be insignificant for the Non-Func sensor, since it exhibited poor sensitivity with poor linearity (data not shown). Therefore, it highlights the limitation of Non-Func sensor towards extremely low concentrations of enteroviral RNA.

Func sensor was also tested for quantification of the enteroviral RNA with the same dilutions. As shown in Fig. 7 A, with increase in concentration of the enteroviral RNA, the PL emission intensity of EB increases gradually. However, a non-linear variation in CDs fluorescence intensity was also observed that complements with the CDs-DNA interaction studies reported in the previous literature [64]. The fluorescence signals from CDs alone do not follow a straightforward pattern, so they cannot be used to measure the concentration of enteroviral RNA accurately. However, when using ratiometric fluorescence intensity, the sensitivity remains high, even at low RNA concentrations. In this study, a linear relationship was found between the ratiometric PL emission intensity of EB and CDs, and the concentration of enteroviral RNA. This linear trend was observed for RNA concentrations ranging from 0.3 nM to 5 nM. The calibration curve of linear regression slope is expressed as y = 0.00443 (nM−1) ∗x + 0.308. The calculated R2 value for Func sensor was documented as high as 0.977 as shown in Fig. 7B. The LOD (Eq. (2)) for target viral RNA was as low as 67 pM. The standard error bars demonstrate the reliability and repeatability of the Func sensor. Therefore, it is evident that Func sensor is extremely sensitive in detecting enteroviral RNA with even such a low concentration.

Additionally, the Func sensor was tested for in-situ detection of enterovirus RNA in real-time in vitro (Fig. 7C–F). During infection, enteroviruses enter the cells via endosomes, where conformational changes of the virus capsid allow the viral RNA to be released from a small hole at the 2-fold axes of symmetry [65]. We have earlier shown that the virus capsid opening and RNA release can be induced in vitro by mimicking the endosomal ionic conditions where the virus normally opens inside cells [66]. Thus, we incubated echovirus 1 in an NK buffer at physiological temperature (37 °C), mimicking the opening conditions, and monitored the PL of the Func sensor, mixed within the same buffer, over 200 min, as shown in the schematics of Fig. 7G. Finally, the temperature was increased to 50 °C for the last two timepoints (240 and 360 min) to confirm full RNA exposure upon virus breakage. To analyze the status of the virus particles during the incubation, DLS was used to evaluate the size distribution in the virus sample at the different timepoints (Fig. 7C). During the first 100 min, a clear peak at around 30 nm was seen, which corresponded to the intact echovirus 1 particle population. However, at later timepoints, a population above 50 nm and finally at around 150 nm appeared, suggesting that the virus particles broke down and hydrophobic areas of the capsid were exposed, causing aggregation of the particles.

More importantly, the IEB/ICDs ratio of the Func sensor fluorescence increased gradually over time, suggesting RNA release from the capsid (Fig. 7D, E and Figs. S16A and B). Furthermore, the fluorescence ratio remained stable if only CDs-Probe and EB were incubated in these conditions without any virus (Fig. S16C), further suggesting a response of the Func sensor to the released RNA. As the fluorescence ratio increased already at the timepoints where intact capsid population with DLS was observed, it can be concluded that the RNA was first released through smaller openings in the capsid which could be detected using the Func sensor. In addition, the ratiometric fluorescence response of the Func sensor almost reached saturation already at the 200 min timepoint, indicating that almost all viral RNA had been released during the incubation at 37 °C, and before more elaborate capsid breakage (Fig. 7F). Our results thus show that the Func sensor can be used to detect the release of enteroviral RNA in real-time in vitro.

If we assume that all the viral RNA has been released from viruses at the end of the experiments, which should be the case, then our final RNA concentration is 0.02 nM. According to our calibration from Fig. 7B this should yield IEB/ICDs ≈ 0.3081, which correspond well to the measured value of 0.3091. Since we are on the lower limit of the linear regime tested in Fig. 7B and can still detect the opening of the virus on the beginning of the experiment of Fig. 7F, we can conclude that our sensor has a potential to be developed even more precise detection.

Additionally, we conducted interference measurements by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of CDs and EB, including the ratio (IEB/ICDs), as shown in Fig. S17. The signal of the Func sensor was measured in the presence and absence of the target sequence in various solutions containing different combinations of amino acids (125 μM), MgCl2 (100 μM), phosphate ions (100 μM), bovine serum albumin (BSA) (3 μg), streptavidin (3 μg), and luciferase (5 μg). The spectra were measured at room temperature using an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

From the graph (Fig. S17C), it is evident that there is no significant variation in the IEB/ICDs ratio in the presence or absence of the target sequence, with the reference being a solution containing only water. The overall fluctuation ranges from 0 to 6 % for Func sensor with the target sequence and 0–2 % for Func sensor without the target sequence. This indicates the practical applicability of the Func sensor in real environments or samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of CDs & CDs-Probe cytotoxicity on mammalian cell line
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the promising applications of CDs and CDs-Probe in biological contexts, it is essential to evaluate their cytotoxicity through a comprehensive cytotoxicity assay. We performed an assay on live A549 cells, with concentration of CDs ranging from 0.1 μM to 500 μM and CDs-Probe from 0.45 μM to 2250 μM, assessed at one time point, 24h. The control includes only A549 cells without the incubation of CDs or CDs-Probe. The cell viability results have been illustrated in SI, S10, Fig. S15.The outcome revealed that after 24 h of incubation with CDs, it showed a noticeable decline in % cell viability of A549 in between 75 % and ∼80 % whereas for CDs-Probe, it was more than 90 %. This is most likely due to the cell internalization of CDs in higher concentration, while the functionalized CDs do not permeabilize cell membranes. The penetration of CDs will likely affect the cell integrity and normal cell functioning. Consequently, CDs-Probe demonstrates good biocompatibility with live A549 cells, highlighting their potential for various biomedical applications.

In summary, we have presented two novel ratiometric fluorosensors composed of CDs mixed and/or functionalized with short single-stranded oligonucleotides (Probe) and EB for the sensitive and selective detection of target sequences resembling the conserved region of the enteroviral RNA. A detailed steady-state and decay profile analyses were performed to study the interaction between CDs and EB to understand the ratiometric response of both the presented sensors. Moreover, stepwise characterization of CDs/Probe (CDs mixed with probe) and CDs-Probe (CDs covalently functionalized with probe) mixed with EB has been carried out systematically to establish the working of proposed sensors. The innovative approach of covalently functionalized CDs with the probes (CDs-Probe) in the Func sensor has led to a highly sensitive and selective sensor for detecting enteroviral RNA. Func sensor resulted in more efficient quenching of CDs due to improved electron transfer efficiency from CDs to EB, as demonstrated by the decay profile analysis. Additionally, the effect of the probe and target sequence with CDs and EB alone was compared, highlighting that the proximity of each component in the Func sensor is crucial for a comprehensive electron transfer process. On the other hand, the Non-Func sensor was not sensitive enough to be considered a highly effective system for real-time monitoring of viral RNA. The enhanced sensitivity and selectivity showcased the capability of the Func sensor and also overcome the limitations of the Non-Func sensor. The statistical data manifest the linear range for the Non-Func sensor from 100 nM to 3 μM (LOD = 88.7 nm). In contrast, the linear range of the Func sensor extended from 0.5 nM to 3 μM with an LOD of 270 pM in the presence of a standard sample containing the short sequence of TS. Moreover, the sensitivity of both sensors was tested with real enteroviral RNA, and the Func sensor was found to be sensitive in quantifying viral RNA, with a linear range of 0.3 nM–5 nM (LOD = 67 pM) whereas Non-func sensor showed no reasonable response, demonstrating the importance of the covalent bonding between CDs and probe. In addition, Func sensor could detect enteroviral RNA release from the capsid in real-time in vitro. Thus, our comparative studies indicate that the Func sensor is a novel viral RNA sensing platform and offers a much-needed possibility to detect real-time viral RNA appearance during infection. However, for future in vivo and clinical applications, less cytotoxic probes are needed.
